verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Athanasius has heard of his own proscription.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Such were the rumours that were noised abroad; and although everything was thus turned upside down, I still did not relinquish my earnest desire of coming to your Piety, but was again setting forward on my journey.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And I did so the more eagerly, being confident that these proceedings were contrary to your wishes, and that if your Grace should be informed of what was done, you would prevent it for the time to come.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: For I could not think that a righteous king could wish Bishops to be banished, and virgins to be stripped, or the Churches to be in any way disturbed.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: While I thus reasoned and hastened on my journey, behold a third report reached me, to the effect that letters had been written to the Princes of Auxumis, desiring that Frumentius, Bishop of Auxumis, should be brought from thence, and that search should be made for me even as far as the country of the Barbarians, that I might be handed over to the Commentaries (as they are called) of the Prefects, and that all the laity and clergy should be compelled to communicate with the Arian heresy, and that such as would not comply with this order should be put to death.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: To shew that these were not merely idle rumours, but that they were confirmed by facts, since your Grace has given me leave, I produce the letter.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: My enemies were constantly reading it, and threatening each one with death.
